anchor.snippet: numerical stability is a generally desirable property of numerical algorithms
Calculations that can be proven not to magnify approximation errors are called numerically stable
The forward error of the algorithm is the difference between the result and the "true" solution
The backward error is the smallest

annotations.label: Forward-backward error bound via condition number

anchor.snippet: the forward error is at most as big in magnitude as the condition number multiplied by the magnitude of the backward error
The algorithm is said to be backward stable if the backward error is small for all inputs
a more general concept, called mixed stability
Hence, a backward stable algorithm is always stable
An algorithm is forward stable if its forward error divided by the condition number of the problem is small
various concepts of numerical stability exist, for instance A-stability

annotations.label: Stability for linear evolutionary PDE algorithms

anchor.snippet: An algorithm for solving a linear evolutionary partial differential equation is stable if the total variation of the numerical solution at a fixed time remains bounded as the step size goes to zero
The Lax equivalence theorem states that an algorithm converges if it is consistent and stable

annotations.label: Babylonian method vs Method X for sqrt(2)
